items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.
Correct answer: SPORT. “FITT” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Tapering. “Overload” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many letters in SPORT principle?
items.why: The real figure is 5 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
